coredata.dc:title: Self-ratiometric fluorescence approach based on room-temperature instantaneously synthesized carbon dots from Folin's reagent and ethanolamine for determination of nitroxinil in water, milk, and food samples
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Abd Elhaleem, Shymaa M.
Belal, Fathalla
El-Shabrawy, Yasser
El-Maghrabey, Mahmoud
coredata.dc:description:
Background Nitroxinil has been used extensively to treat parasitic worms, mainly Fasciola, in food-producing cattle and sheep. The reported methods for nitroxinil analysis included expensive instrumentation, the need for skilled operators, and tedious procedures. Fluorimetry is one of the fastest and simplest methods widely used; hence, we aimed to develop a simple, cost-effective, and convenient fluorometric approach for the estimation of nitroxinil in various matrices. Compared with other detection methods, self-ratiometric fluorescent probes are considered a promising approach for the detection of analytes as their detection accuracy overcomes traditional fluorescence sensing probe in that it is not affected by the probe concentration, solution polarity, instrument parameters, and other factors. In this research, room temperature instantaneously synthesized carbon dots were used as a sensitive and selective self-ratiometric probe for the determination of the veterinary medicine nitroxinil in various matrices.
Results A room-temperature synthesized quinone-ethanolamine carbon dots (RTQECDs) was fabricated using the instantaneous reaction of sodium 1,2-naphthoquinone-4-sulfonate (Folin's) with ethanolamine, without any energy/catalyzing reagents, for the first time. The prepared carbon dots show green-blue fluorescence at 450 nm upon exposure to UV light at 365 nm with a quantum yield of 26.6 %. Upon interaction with nitroxinil, the fluorescence intensity of RTQECDs at 450 nm is quenched and shifted to a longer wavelength at 475 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of RTQECDs at 400 nm (absorbance maxima of nitroxinil) was more extremely quenched under the same conditions. Taking this in hand, a new RTQECDs self-ratiometric probe was developed for the determination of nitroxinil using the decrease in peaks at 450 nm and 400 nm and the shift of the fluorescence maxima to 475 nm as built-in reference peaks. The probe showed a quantitative increase in signal output of F475/F400 in the range of 0.10–30.0 μg/mL nitroxinil with a limit of detection of 30.0 ppb. The nitroxinil-sensing mechanism using RTQECDs is mainly ascribed to the partial secondary blue-type inner filter effect (IFE). The designed study was applied for the estimation of nitroxinil in veterinary dosage forms (recoveries; 99.78 %–100.35 %), river water (recoveries; 98.55 %–101.53 %), and food products, including meat, liver, kidney, and milk (recoveries; 97.60 %–104.25 %).
Significance The novelty of our work includes the immediate synthesis of the sensing probe at room temperature, as well as its use as a self-ratiometric fluorescence probe for the determination of nitroxinil in veterinary samples, river water, and food products with excellent sensitivity down to 30.0 ppb. RTQECDs own the highest response and selectivity to nitroxinil compared with cations, anions, as well as other co-administered drugs, including cefotaxime and ivermectin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Self-ratiometric fluorescence probe
Naphthoquinone-sulfonate
Ethanolamine
Nitroxinil
Food products and environmental water

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of NX in veterinary preparations
Analysis of NX in water samples
Analysis of NX in meat, kidney, and liver samples
Self-ratiometric sensing of NX and mechanism of fluorescence sensor
Linear range and limit of detection (LOD)
Analysis of NX in dosage forms
Analysis of NX in milk, meat, kidney, and liver

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is funded by Mansoura University (project code number: Mu-phar-23\u201332).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of combination between sodium 1,2-naphthoquinone-4-sulfonate and ethanolamine on QY in (A) the absence of l-cysteine (inset: RTQECDs at normal light(left) and RTQECDs at UV light(right)), and (B) presence of l-cysteine (inset: CDs at normal light(left) and CDs at UV light(right)).

(A): Typical SEM image of RTQECDs, (B): Typical TEM image of RTQECDs and their size distribution (from 1 to 3.5 nm), (C): EDX spectrum of as-synthesized RTQECDs, (D): Elemental mapping of RTQECDs, (E): FT-IR spectrum of RTQECDs, (F): A co-plot showing great overlap between UV–Vis absorption spectrum of NX [20.0 μg/mL] and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of RTQECDs (inset: RTQECDs at normal light (A), RTQECDs at UV light (B), and RTQECDs upon addition of NX at UV light (C)), (G): Fluorescence emission spectra of RTQECDs at 450 nm with different excitation wavelengths, and (H): The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of RTQECDs.

(A): Fluorescence emission spectra of RTQECDs in aqueous solution upon the addition of various concentrations of NX (from top to bottom: 0 μg/mL, 0.1 μg/mL, 0.5 μg/mL, 10.0 μg/mL, 15.0 μg/mL, 20.0 μg/mL, 25.0 μg/mL, 30.0 μg/mL), (B) and (C): show the calibration plots of NX through plotting of F475/F450 and F475/F400, respectively on the y-axis vs. NX concentrations (μg/mL) on the x-axis, (D): shows a calibration plot between F0/F versus NX concentrations (μg/mL), (E): Linear regression plot (n = 3) of the quenched fluorescence intensity (ΔF) at 450 nm and NX concentration in the range of 0.1–30.0 μg/mL, (F): Suppressed efficiency of the observed and corrected fluorescence of RTQECDs after the addition of different concentrations of NX (0.1 μg/mL, 0.5 μg/mL, 10.0 μg/mL, 15.0 μg/mL, 20.0 μg/mL, 25.0 μg/mL, 30.0 μg/mL). Every experiment was repeated at least three times where the shown results are average, and the error bars represent the SD.
Selectivity and interference of RTQECDs towards NX in the presence of other common species. The fluorescence intensities of RTQECDs without and with NX at the ratio F475/F400, respectively.
General procedure for synthesis and application of RTQECDs as a self-ratiometric probe.
General illustration for the quenching mechanism of RTQECDs in the presence of NX.
Comparison between the proposed room temperature method and other strategies for the synthesis of CDs.

row.entry: Glucose or sucrose or starch + nitric acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the reported methods and the proposed method for NX detection.

row.entry: Milk, kidney, liver, and meat samples
River water, Milk, kidney, liver, and meat samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of the proposed fluorescence sensor for NX determination in river water and food samples and comparison with a reference method [13].

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each result is the average of three separate determinations.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values between parentheses are the tabulated t and F values at P = 0.05.

head.ce:title: Self-ratiometric fluorescence approach based on room-temperature instantaneously synthesized carbon dots from Folin's reagent and ethanolamine for determination of nitroxinil in water, milk, and food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, 35516, Mansoura, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, 35516, Mansoura, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences, Course of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Nagasaki University, 1-14 Bunkyo-machi, Nagasaki, 852-8521, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences, Course of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Nagasaki University, 1-14 Bunkyo-machi, Nagasaki, 852-8521, Japan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, 35516, Mansoura, Egypt.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitroxinil has been used extensively to treat parasitic worms, mainly Fasciola, in food-producing cattle and sheep. The reported methods for nitroxinil analysis included expensive instrumentation, the need for skilled operators, and tedious procedures. Fluorimetry is one of the fastest and simplest methods widely used; hence, we aimed to develop a simple, cost-effective, and convenient fluorometric approach for the estimation of nitroxinil in various matrices. Compared with other detection methods, self-ratiometric fluorescent probes are considered a promising approach for the detection of analytes as their detection accuracy overcomes traditional fluorescence sensing probe in that it is not affected by the probe concentration, solution polarity, instrument parameters, and other factors. In this research, room temperature instantaneously synthesized carbon dots were used as a sensitive and selective self-ratiometric probe for the determination of the veterinary medicine nitroxinil in various matrices.

A room-temperature synthesized quinone-ethanolamine carbon dots (RTQECDs) was fabricated using the instantaneous reaction of sodium 1,2-naphthoquinone-4-sulfonate (Folin's) with ethanolamine, without any energy/catalyzing reagents, for the first time. The prepared carbon dots show green-blue fluorescence at 450 nm upon exposure to UV light at 365 nm with a quantum yield of 26.6 %. Upon interaction with nitroxinil, the fluorescence intensity of RTQECDs at 450 nm is quenched and shifted to a longer wavelength at 475 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of RTQECDs at 400 nm (absorbance maxima of nitroxinil) was more extremely quenched under the same conditions. Taking this in hand, a new RTQECDs self-ratiometric probe was developed for the determination of nitroxinil using the decrease in peaks at 450 nm and 400 nm and the shift of the fluorescence maxima to 475 nm as built-in reference peaks. The probe showed a quantitative increase in signal output of F475/F400 in the range of 0.10–30.0 μg/mL nitroxinil with a limit of detection of 30.0 ppb. The nitroxinil-sensing mechanism using RTQECDs is mainly ascribed to the partial secondary blue-type inner filter effect (IFE). The designed study was applied for the estimation of nitroxinil in veterinary dosage forms (recoveries; 99.78 %–100.35 %), river water (recoveries; 98.55 %–101.53 %), and food products, including meat, liver, kidney, and milk (recoveries; 97.60 %–104.25 %).

The novelty of our work includes the immediate synthesis of the sensing probe at room temperature, as well as its use as a self-ratiometric fluorescence probe for the determination of nitroxinil in veterinary samples, river water, and food products with excellent sensitivity down to 30.0 ppb. RTQECDs own the highest response and selectivity to nitroxinil compared with cations, anions, as well as other co-administered drugs, including cefotaxime and ivermectin.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the ideal candidates for nanotechnology's contribution is carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs). CQDs exhibit high water solubility, low cytotoxicity, good chemical stability, abundant resources, and luminescence features. CQDs can be employed in various applications, including sensors, photovoltaic devices, extraction beds, and bio-imaging technology [1,2]. The functional groups are extremely valuable for carbon dots' properties, such as photoluminescence and water solubility, and give them the ability to be conjugated with other molecules. Furthermore, modifications, including heteroatom doping and functionalization, can be easily done during CQDs syntheses or post-treatments [3–5]. Our scientific research team shows a keen interest in manufacturing carbon dots (CDs) owing to their incredible and amazing merits and makes good use of them in the analytical assay of various analytes.

Parasite infections can lead to serious health problems for livestock, as infected animals exhibit numerous signs of diarrhea, loss of appetite, and hypoproteinemia [6]. Anthelmintics such as nitroxinil (NX) are required for helminth control. NX (4-hydroxy-3-iodo-5-nitrobenzonitrile) belongs to the group of phenolic substitutes that are effective against several types of parasites, including cestode and trematode. To avoid economic losses in animal husbandry, NX is widely used to prevent and control fascioliasis in sheep and food-producing cattle. Serious adverse effects on human health, such as fever, tachycardia, respiratory irritation, and allergic skin reactions, can occur as a result of excessive consumption of food products containing NX. The European Commission Regulations (EC No. 997/1999) recommended that the maximum NX residue in animal muscle products should be less than 400 μg/kg in order to ensure food safety [7].

Currently, numerous analytical methods for the qualitative and quantitative detection of NX have been developed, for instance, HPLC [8–10] and voltammetry [11,12], but all of these techniques include expensive instrumentation, the need for skilled operators, intricate sample pre-treatment and tedious procedures. In contrast, steady-state fluorimetry is one of the fastest and simplest methods widely used for NX detection [13–15]. Hence, there is an urgent need to develop a simple, cost-effective, and convenient approach for the quantitative estimation of NX in solution. Compared with other detection methods, self-ratiometry is considered a promising approach for the detection of analytes as its detection accuracy overcomes traditional fluorescence of the sensing probe in that it is not affected by the probe concentration, solution polarity, instrument parameters, and other factors [16]. Self-ratiometric approach is based on self-calibration, which provides a more reliable analysis by varying the fluorescence intensity ratio [17].

In the present work, the combination of the intrinsic optical merits of the room-temperature synthesized quinone-ethanolamine carbon dots (RTQECDs) and the advantages of the ratiometric fluorescent method can eliminate instrumental or environmental interferences. We fabricated a stable and reliable RTQECDs-based self-ratiometric fluorescent probe for accurate quantitative NX sensing, applying built-in correction behavior and background-free property.

Our scientific research team was focusing on using quinones in versatile applications [18–20], and we also have a keen interest in the green synthesis of carbon dots (CDs). In our approach, CDs-based luminophores were successfully fabricated through the immediate interaction of novel substrates, including sodium 1,2-naphthoquinone-4-sulfonate (Folin's reagent) and ethanolamine at room temperature for the first time without any external energy source. The quantum yield was also investigated in the absence and presence of another source of S- atoms (l-cysteine). Specifically, the prepared sensor provides a more sensitive detection due to its outstanding properties. The highly fluorescent RTQECDs were clearly quenched by NX, facilitating their detection in different matrices, including veterinary formulations, water samples, and food samples (meat, kidney, liver, and milk). Different experimental parameters have been optimized, including the volume of RTQECDs, pH effect, and incubation time. Furthermore, the sensing properties of the prepared dots and their possible sensing mechanism were deeply discussed in detail. The application of the sensitized self-ratiometric fluorescence probe provided a new research basis for designing and enhancing efficiency. In comparison to the previous works, our study reflects simple, rapid, cost-effective, high stability, strong salt tolerance, less time-consuming, and higher sensitivity analysis of NX.

Phosphoric acid, acetic acid, boric acid, sodium hydroxide (used for preparation Britton-Robinson buffer), glucose, lactose, l-cysteine and metal salts (sodium chloride, potassium chloride, magnesium chloride, nickel chloride, cobalt (II) chloride, and cadmium sulfate, barium chloride, sodium sulfite, sodium thiosulphate, and sodium sulfide) were obtained from Sigma - Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany. Nitroxinil (B.N.0000124001) with 99.80 % purity was kindly supplied by Natco Lab Chemical Reagents Co. Cairo, Egypt. Dovix® injectable solution (Batch No. 0607/23) containing 25 gm NX/100 mL, product of Arabcomed Veterinary Co., Cairo, Egypt, was obtained from a local veterinary clinic. Real water samples used in this study were collected locally from Nile River in Mansoura City, Egypt. For all experiments, all solutions were prepared using ultra-pure water, and all chemicals were of Analytical Grade, purchased from commercial suppliers, and no further purification was required.

The UV–vis absorption spectra were performed on a spectrometer (Shimadzu-1601). Fluorescence spectra measurements were performed at room temperature on a spectrofluorometer (Cary Eclipse). The excitation wavelength was 340 nm, and the slit width for the excitation and emission was 5 nm. A centrifuge (model 2-16P, SIGMA, Germany) was utilized. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX, Oxford-Xmax 20) was applied for elemental composition and surface morphology characterization of the sensing probe. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were monitored on a transmission microscope (JEOL, JSM -2100-Tokyo, Japan). A scanning electron microscope FEG-SEM (Thermo Scientific, Massachusetts, United States) was used to detect the morphology of the prepared dots. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were scanned and recorded on a (IS10 Nicolet) spectrometer with KBr spheres (USA) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1.

Britton-Robinson buffer (BRB) was prepared by mixing 0.04 M boric acid, 0.04 M acetic acid, and 0.04 M phosphoric acid solutions. NaOH solution (0.2 M) was used to adjust the pH. A standard solution of NX (0.5 mg/mL) was prepared in methanol. Serial dilutions were made using ultra-pure water to get working solutions in the range of 0.10–30.0 μg/mL. The stock solution of NX was stored in the fridge and was found to remain stable for at least two weeks.

In general, 100 mg of 1,2-naphthoquinone-4-sulfonate was dissolved in 6 mL of pure water and then ultrasonicated for 2 min. Subsequently, 1 mL of ethanolamine was added and homogeneously mixed, resulting in a dark brown solution of RTQECDs immediately obtained within a few seconds. For analytical use, 6 mL of the self-ratiometric fluorescent probe was transferred after purification by centrifugation and filtration with a syringe filter (0.45 μm) into a 100-mL volumetric flask and then completed to the mark with water. The prepared solution was kept and stored in refrigerator (4 °C) for further use. Scheme 1 illustrates the fabrication process of RTQECDs and sensing applications.

The quantum yield (QY) of RTQECDs was measured using quinine sulfate as a reference standard (λex = 350 nm, Qs = 54 %) in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution based on the following formula:
(1)
Q x
=
Q s
×
I x
I s
×
A s
A x
×
(
η x
η s
)
2
Where subscript s, x, Q, η
, I, and A refer to the referemce material, the analyte, QY, refractive index, emission peak integration area, and absorbance, respectively [21].

Initially, different concentrations of NX solution were transferred into a series of 10-mL volumetric flasks containing 35 μL of RTQECDs, and then pure water was added to the mark. After mixing, the emission intensity was measured at 400, 450, and 475 nm after excitation at 340 nm. Parallel measurements were conducted three times to ensure the accuracy of the experimental results. The fluorescence intensity ratio F475/F400 of the dots was plotted vs. concentrations of NX (μg/mL) to attain the calibration graph, and then the corresponding regression equation was derived.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of NX in veterinary preparations
ce:section.ce:para: A certain volume was transferred from Dovix® injectable solution into a 100-mL volumetric flask to attain a claimed concentration of (1 mg/mL). The final volume was completed using methanol and then sonicated for 10 min. Subsequently, three increasing concentrations of NX were prepared and then assayed as expressed in section “2.6” to determine the nominal content and % recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of NX in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In general, environmental samples (river water) were collected from Nile River (Mansoura City) in September 2023 to estimate the ability of the probe to detect NX. At a distance of 10 m from the shore, a glass or polypropylene bottle was used to collect water, the lid was opened at a distance of 50 cm from the water's surface, and it was covered under water to avoid any change in the characteristics of the sample obtained. The samples were transferred to the lab in a cooled container for analysis. Then, the water samples were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min, and the supernatant was filtered with 0.45 μm membrane to remove the suspended particles before further analysis. Subsequently, several concentrations of NX standard solution were spiked to the filtered samples. The experimental procedure was performed following the same steps as under section “2.6”.

Milk samples were obtained from a local supermarket. Each sample (1.0 mL) of milk was transferred into 15-mL centrifuge tubes and then spiked with various aliquots of NX standard solution. The mixture was vortex-mixed, followed by the addition of 4.0 mL of methanol. Centrifugation was operated at 4800 rpm for 20 min. Finally, after centrifugation, the supernatant was filtered by a syring filter of 0.45 μm, and 1 mL of the filtrate was transferred into a series of 10-mL volumetric flasks. 35 μL Aliquot of RTQECDs solution was introduced, and the experimental steps under section “2.6” were followed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of NX in meat, kidney, and liver samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the applicability of the developed approach, the sensing probe was used to analyze NX in some food samples. Commercial meat, liver, and kidney samples were bought from the local market. The samples were homogenized and kept on ice while transported to the laboratory. Weigh accurately about 0.5 gm of each sample, then transfer it into a series of 15-mL centrifuge tubes. Different aliquots of the drug were added, then vortex mixed for 2 min. Methanol was added up to 5 mL and then vortex mixed for 1 min, then centrifuged at 4800 rpm for 20 min. The obtained supernatant was filtered by a 0.45 μm syringe filter. One mL aliquot of the filtrate was transferred into 10-mL volumetric flasks utilizing methanol as a diluting solvent. The previous experimental steps under section “2.6” were followed. The FL spectra were recorded at a detector sensitivity of 780 voltage from 360 to 600 nm upon excitation light at 340 nm.

Firstly, we intended to fabricate high-quantum yield dots at room temperature without the need for any external energy supply, which would significantly reduce energy consumption. Therefore, we prepared two different quantum dots batches from sodium 1,2-naphthoquinone-4-sulfonate as a source of C and S atoms and ethanolamine as a source of both N and C atoms in presence and absence of l-cysteine (250 mg/3 mL ultra-pure water) as another source for S atoms hoping to increase the QY. It was found that the QY was 16 % with blue fluorescence after the addition of l-cysteine, while it was 26.6 % with green-blue fluorescence in the absence of l-cysteine reflecting that incorporation of l-cysteine with other starting precursors, resulting in a noticeable decrease in QY. This behavior may be attributed to the assumption that l-cysteine affected Schiff's base crosslinking and oxidation polymerization reaction between sodium 1,2-naphthoquinone-4-sulfonate and ethanolamine that yielded the CDs (Fig. 1 ).

Hence, sodium 1,2-naphthoquinone-4-sulfonate and ethanolamine were selected to complement our study and were carefully mixed to obtain green-blue fluorescent RTQECDs. Both 1,2-naphthoquinone-4-sulfonate (chromogenic reagent) and ethanolamine have no fluorescence, and this was practically confirmed, as indicated in Fig. S1. CDs are formed by the imine bond crosslinking between the precursor molecules. The carbonyl (C=O) group of sodium 1,2-naphthoquinone-4-sulfonate can react with the NH2 group of ethanolamine by Schiff base condensation at room temperature to form a crosslinked network structure, which results in the further formation of CDs. Therefore, the formation of CDs is directly attributed to the imine bond crosslinking between sodium 1,2-naphthoquinone-4-sulfonate and ethanolamine. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to analyze the morphology of the prepared dots. As shown in Fig. 2 A, RTQECDs showed smooth spherical nano-aggregates due to their ultra small size [22]. The size of RTQECDs was observed by the application of a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM). The HR-TEM image illustrates the monodispersity of the dots, and the associated histogram is presented, revealing that CD sizes are perfectly dispersed between 1 and 3.5 nm, with an average diameter of 2 nm, meaning that the CDs have a high quantum confinement effect, as indicated in Fig. 2B. To further analyze the elemental composition of RTQECDs, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was conducted. As apparent in Fig. 2C, RTQECDs consist of high concentrations of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, as well as trace amounts of sulfur. The high oxygen concentration reveals the opportunity for the presence of different functional groups (such as carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups) on the surface of the prepared dots. Simultaneously, the element mapping images (Fig. 2D) imply that RTQECDS only contained C, N, S, and O elements, which were homogeneously distributed on the surface of the dots, confirming EDX analyses. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometry was utilized for further analysis of the functional groups on the surface of RTQECDs. As revealed in Fig. 2E, the characteristic broad peak at 3437 cm−1 refers to N–H and O–H stretching vibrations, while a sharp peak at 1641 cm−1 represents the C=C bond. In addition, the peaks of C–H stretching vibration (555 and 2932 cm−1), C=O vibration (1450 cm−1), C–N band (2100 cm−1), and C=C sharp peak (1590 cm−1) were also presented in the spectra of RTQECDs. In addition, the peak at 1317 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of C–S, while the bond at 1650 cm−1 region is related to imine (–C=N), which is attributed to the Schiff base reaction [23,24].

In order to get more insights into the optical properties of RTQECDs, the dark brown solution of the dots was exposed to UV light of 365 nm indicating green-blue fluorescence emitting property of the dots (Fig. 2F). As exhibited in (Fig. 2G), the fluorescence emission spectra of RTQECDs were obtained at 450 nm with a variety of excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 400 nm, exhibits an obvious excitation-independent emission behavior of the dots. Overall, the high fluorescence emission of RTQECDs was attained at an excitation of 340 nm (Fig. 2F). The emission intensity of RTQECDs was dependent on the variation of pH over the range of 3–11. From Fig. 2H, in an acidic solution, a significant decrease in fluorescence was obvious, probably because the hydroxyl groups (-OH) tended to be unionized, resulting in decreased negative charges and aggregation of RTQECDs with an accompanying decrease in their fluorescence. Conversely, at higher pH values, the emission intensity remains stable, reflecting the presence of hydroxyl groups in the ionized form [25].

The quantum yield (QY) of the room temperature-fabricated dots was found to be 26.6 %, which is superior to most reports [26–32]. Additionally, the time required for the synthesis of RTQECDs took only a few seconds, which made the preparation method superior to other published methods that took several minutes [31,32], hours [28–30], and days [26,27] to prepare, as indicated in Table 1 .

The most significant factors that can affect the sensor performance were optimized to carry out the experimental measurements under preferable conditions. Firstly, the effect of the added volume of RTQECDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. S2A, different volumes of the prepared dots were studied, including (5, 15, 25, 35, 45, 55, and 65 μL). It was deduced that the ratio F475/F400 remains constant from 15 to 65 μL, and hence, 35 μL was selected as the optimum volume of RTQECDs for further study. Moreover, the effect of pH was studied. BRB with various pH values (3, 5, 7, 9, and 11) was used during this study, demonstrating that no significant effect was observed before and after the addition of BRB of pH values 7, 9, and 11, as displayed in Fig. S2B. The ratio F475/F400 of RTQECDs after the addition of NX at pH 3 and 5 decreased. This reveals that the sensor functions best in the absence of a buffer or at a pH range of 7–11. Hence, the next experiment was carried out in the absence of a buffer. Additionally, the influence of incubation time from 1 min to 30 min was further examined. The results of the time-response study indicated that the probe had no time-response for NX recognition, reflecting that NX is bound completely with the active sites of the sensing probe and remained stable for up to 30 min (Fig. S2C). Therefore, 1 min was selected as the optimum incubation time for our designed study. These results displayed that the sensing probe was a “rapid response” FL probe for NX detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-ratiometric sensing of NX and mechanism of fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The room temperature-synthesized RTQECDs exhibit an emission peak at 450 nm upon excitation at 340 nm. After the addition of increasing concentrations of NX, the fluorescence intensity of RTQECDs at 450 nm decreased and red-shifted to a longer wavelength of 475 nm. RTQECDs fluorescence is also quenched at 450 (emission maxima of blank RTQECDs) and at 400 nm (maximum absorbance wavelength of NX) in a concentration-dependent manner. Hence, a self-ratiometric strategy was developed for the estimation of NX depending on the fluorescence quenching of the prepared dots with a red shift from 450 nm to 475 nm. The red shift in the maximum emission peak to 475 nm in the presence of increasing concentrations of NX is attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE). This phenomenon is generally known as IFE-induced concentration-dependent redshift (CDRS). This phenomenon occurs due to the partial overlap between the emission spectrum of the fluorophore (RTQECDs) and the absorption spectrum of the quencher (NX). This overlap occurs due to the absorption of radiation from the region with shorter wavelengths (blue side) of the fluorescence emission peak. Hence, the name of the mechanism is the blue-sided partial secondary IFE mechanism.

Subsequently, the peaks at 450 nm and 475 nm represented reference peaks for self-ratimetric analysis of NX in addition to the fluorescence intensity at 400 nm (maximum absorbance wavelength of NX). To obtain a calibration plot, the emission intensity at 475 nm is divided by the fluorescence intensity at 450 or 400 nm versus the concentration of NX (μg/mL). Different concentrations of NX (0.10–30.0 μg/mL) were mixed with RTQECDs, and fluorescence measurements were performed after excitation at 340 nm, as indicated in Fig. 3 A. The fluorescence ratios of F475/F450 and F475/F400 were plotted versus NX concentrations to obtain two calibration curves using the FI at 400 nm and 475 nm as dual self-reference (Fig. 3B and C). Additionally, two calibration plots were obtained by plotting F0/F or ΔF at 450 nm against NX concentrations, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of RTQECDs in the absence and presence of NX at 450 nm (Fig. 3D and E). It was found that the coefficient of the determination when using F475/F450 and F475/F400 was 0.9651 and 0.9965, respectively. While using F0/F and ΔF450 as a response, the coefficient of the determination was found to be 0.9775 and 0.9933, respectively. It obviously noted that the ratiometric probe improved the linearity of the analytical response. The best results were obtained using the fluorescence intensity ratio F475/F400 as the analytical response (Fig. 3C), and it was applied in further studies.

The fluorescence response of the room temperature-synthesized RTQECDs to NX molecules was explored, deducing that the emission intensity of the sensing probe was gradually decreased upon the addition of increasing concentrations of NX in the range of 0.10–30.0 μg/mL, as illustrated in Fig. 3A. The ratio of F475/F400 was plotted versus NX concentration (μg/mL) with a linear correlation of (r = 0.9983), as demonstrated in Fig. 3C. The sensor response mechanisms can be classified as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), aggregation-induced emission (AIE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and inner filter effect (IFE). To deeply understand the potential mechanism of the fluorescence response of RTQECDs with NX, the absorbance band of NX and the excitation and emission spectra of RTQECDs solution were performed to examine their degree of overlap. Clearly, there was considerable spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of NX and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of RTQECDs, which implied that, possibly, the mechanism of quenching could be IFE or FRET, as illustrated in Fig. 2F.

To confirm that the interaction between RTQECDs and NX happens via the IFE phenomenon and to understand the mechanism by which NX quenches RTQECDs fluorescence, correction of the emission intensity of RTQECDs upon addition of NX was performed by applying the following equation [33,34]:
(2)
F
Corr
=
F Obs
X
10
A Ex
+
A Em
2
Where,
FObs and FCorr are the observed and corrected fluorescence intensities, respectively.
AEm and AEx refer to the absorbance at the emission wavelength (450 nm) and the maximum excitation wavelength (340 nm), respectively.

Subsequently, the fluorescence suppression efficacy (%E) for both observed and corrected fluorescence was computed according to Eq. 3
(3)
% E =
[
1 −
(
F
F O
)
]
x 100

The curve of %E versus the concentration of NX (μg/mL) was plotted (Fig. 3F). Obviously, the spectral overlap gives NX a clear IFE (Scheme 2 ) on the fluorescence of RTQECDs since the %E is completely lost for Fcorr, as shown in Fig. 3F. Scheme 2 illustrates the reaction mechanism between RTQECDs and NX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear range and limit of detection (LOD)
ce:section.ce:para: The developed method was evaluated with respect to ICH Q2R1 recommendations [35]. Under the optimum conditions, the emission intensity of RTQECDs was gradually decreased upon the addition of NX over a linear range (0.10–30.0 μg/mL). We found that the presence of NX could induce a specific ratiometric fluorescence response in the long-wavelength emission region of the RTQECDs. As shown in Fig. 3C, the self-ratiometric fluorescent intensity F475/F400 (F475 and F400 denoted the emission intensity under 340 nm excitation) of the fluorescent sensor gradually enhanced with the increase of NX concentration with linear regression equation of F475/F400 = 0.0425 [NX] + 1.995 (R2 = 0.9965), enabling accurate and reliable response toward NX. (Table S1). The limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) were calculated and found to be 0.030 μg/mL (30.0 ppb) and 0.092 μg/mL, respectively. The LOQ and LOD were calculated utilizing the corresponding equations 10 (Sa/b) and 3.3 (Sa/b), respectively (where Sa = standard deviation of 10 parallel measurements for a blank solution and b = slope). Our designed system exhibited higher sensitivity than the HPLC- UV method, which uses expensive solvents and UV detection at 270 nm, making it susceptible to interference [8]. The reported voltammetric methods use expensive equipment and materials in addition to their low sensitivity compared to our developed method [11,12]. Additionally, this method shows similar sensitivity to another spectrofluorimetric method using a flavonoid dye prepared in a complex synthetic manner over a long period, unlike our fabricated sensor that was immediately prepared at room temperature [14]. Regarding the recently reported methods, the spectrofluorimetric method shows very high sensitivity but requires a very complex procedure, including a reduction reaction with Zn/HCl for 20 min [13]. LC-MS technique also investigates NX at higher sensitivity; however, it requires an experienced analyst to treat it in addition to expensive instrumentation [10]. Table 2 indicates the comparison between the reported methods and our proposed method.

Additionally, the greenness of the developed method was evaluated, and the designed system was found to express a high degree of greenness according to several tools, including (Complex-GAPI, AGREE-prep, and AGREE), as described in detail in the Supplementary material Section S1 and (Figs. S3A–C).

For investigation of method accuracy, it was essential to compare the results of the raw material and the veterinary formulations with those acquired using the comparison method. This approach was carried out in several complex steps, starting with the preparation of a methanolic standard solution of NX, followed by reduction of the nitro group to an amino group using both Zn/HCl allowing the reaction to last for 20 min. Next, filtration was performed to obtain the final stock of the fluorescent derivative that was measured at both 302 nm and 364 nm upon excitation at λ = 277 nm) [13]. Using both the Student t-test and Variance ratio F-test [36], it was deduced that there were no considerable differences between the developed and comparison methods regarding accuracy and precision (Table S2).

To verify the precision of the proposed method, three different concentrations of NX solutions (10.0, 20.0, and 25.0 μg/mL) were measured three times on the same day (intra-day precision) and on three consecutive days (inter-day precision). As indicated in Table S3, the % RSD values are less than 2 %, demonstrating the high precision of the designed study.

The anti-interference ability is an essential parameter for evaluating whether RTQECDs can be utilized to estimate NX in real samples. The selectivity of the prepared dots to NX is firstly attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE) based on the fact that not all drugs cause IFE. Secondly, all measurements were performed around 400 nm; hence, not all drugs or strange substances could interfere during the study. Thirdly, the applied technique (self-ratiometry) increases the selectivity toward NX. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of the self-ratiometric probe in the presence of each of lactose, glucose, S2O3 2−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, CH3COO−, Cl−, K+, Na+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, and Co2+ were studied at concentrations 100 times higher than that of NX. The ratio F475/F400 only increased significantly in the presence of NX, indicating a strong interaction between NX and RTQECDs as well as good selectivity and anti-interference ability, as shown in Fig. 4 . Even high concentration of various metals and species did not cause obvious changes toward the probe, confirming the probe selectivity. The anti-interference ability of RTQECDs against common co-administered drugs, including cefotaxime and ivermectin, was studied. Higher values of tolerance limit for both cefotaxime (150 μg/mL) and ivermectin (200 μg/mL) were obtained, indicating higher selectivity of the fluorescent probe to NX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of NX in dosage forms
ce:section.ce:para: The developed system was applied to estimate NX in Dovix® injectable veterinary solution. From Table S4, the method exhibited high percent recoveries between (98.9 % and 101.0 %) and low %RSD, confirming the ability of our study to determine the studied drug in its veterinary dosage forms efficiently.

Nitroxinil can be released into the environment directly with the feces and urine of animals in pastures. Once released into the environment, these pollutants are transported and distributed into waters [37,38]. Hence, it is essential to detect NX in environmental water samples. The feasibility of the fluorescent probe for the estimation of NX in river water was investigated. The spiking recovery method was applied to detect the added amount of NX in river water samples. Evidently, the results in Table 3 demonstrate that the recoveries acquired by the proposed method are between 98.55 % and 101.53 % with an RSD of 1.51 %, confirming the ability of the designed methodology to assay NX in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of NX in milk, meat, kidney, and liver
ce:section.ce:para: Perusal of literature reports revealed that NX has been used as an anthelmintic in veterinary medicine. Earlier, European Commission Regulation No. 997/1999 had set the regulatory limits (RLs) of NX for the validation of the experimental, analytical methods in bovine milk, fat, muscle, kidney, and liver of 20, 200, 400, 400, and 20 μg kg−1 respectively. Residues of NX in food products are of deep concern because they may be the cause of allergic reactions in sensitive individuals or are involved in the growth of pathogenic bacterial strains that are resistant to antibiotics [7]. Therefore, to ensure food safety, it is important to monitor the concentration of NX in milk samples and other edible tissues of animals treated with the drug. The results indicated that the prepared sensor was suitable for the detection of NX in food samples. The results of recovery tests for spiked NX in milk were between (97.6–104.25 %), meat (97.8–103 %), kidney (98–102.6 %), and liver (98–102.7 %). Obviously, these results revealed that the probe has good results for estimating NX in food products and comparable to the reference method [13] (Table 3 ).

In summary, simple, fast, time-saving, and cost-effective synthesis of RTQECDs has been designed as a self-ratiometric fluorescent probe for analysis of NX. In the present sensing system, the emission signal of RTQECDs was efficiently quenched upon the addition of NX at 400 and 450 nm, and the maxima was shifted to 475 nm, allowing the use of this phenomenon for developing a self-ratiometric probe. The signal (F475/F400 ) of RTQECDs was linearly increased upon the addition of NX over the range 0.10–30.0 μg/mL through the blue-sided partial secondary IFE mechanism. Under optimum experimental factors, RTQECDs display higher sensitivity, stability, and selectivity toward the investigation of NX against various co-administered drugs, other compounds, and metals. In addition, the developed ratiometric sensor represents a considerable step towards the complete quantification of NX in different matrices, such as veterinary samples, river water, milk, meat, kidney, and liver samples. In conclusion, this work provided a fast and accurate sensing scheme for NX estimation in complex matrices. Moreover, the greenness of the developed system was assured by numerous developed tools such as complex GAPI, AGREE, and AGREE-prep.
The authors declare no competing conflicts of interest.
This research did not involve human or animal samples.
This work is funded by Mansoura University (project code number: Mu-phar-23–32).
Authors agree to make data and materials supporting the results or analyses presented in their paper available upon reasonable request.

Shymaa M. Abd Elhaleem: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Fathalla Belal: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Software, Resources, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yasser El-Shabrawy: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization. Mahmoud El-Maghrabey: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
